# A deploy claim holder is a de-facto temporary environment owner: it can
# `deploy grant` a scoped window to someone else without being the pipeline's
# declared EnvironmentOwners entry or an admin — "holding == owning, for exactly
# as long as you hold it."
env BREEZE_DIR=$WORK/state

exec breeze identity register admin
cp stdout admin.token
exec breeze identity register mallory
cp stdout mallory.token
exec breeze identity register bob
cp stdout bob.token

exec breeze role assign deployer mallory --as admin --token-file admin.token
exec breeze apply -f pipeline.hcl --as admin --token-file admin.token

# mallory is not staging's declared owner (nobody is, here) and not an admin —
# a grant attempt before claiming anything is rejected.
! exec breeze deploy grant release --env staging --to bob --ttl 5m --as mallory --token-file mallory.token
stderr 'only environment .* declared owner|admin|currently holding a deploy claim'

# mallory claims the deploy stage's exclusivity for staging.
exec breeze deploy claim release deploy --env staging --as mallory --token-file mallory.token
stdout 'lock l1'

# Now mallory, while holding that claim, CAN grant bob a scoped window.
exec breeze deploy grant release --env staging --to bob --ttl 5m --as mallory --token-file mallory.token
stdout 'granted bob access to release/staging'

# Release mallory's own claim first — it's exclusive, so bob's actual deploy
# would otherwise fail on a lock conflict regardless of RBAC/grant correctness.
exec breeze lock release l1 --as mallory

# bob can now deploy on the strength of the grant alone, despite lacking the
# deployer role and never being a declared owner.
exec breeze stage start release deploy abc123 --env staging --as bob --token-file bob.token
stdout 'deploy: succeeded'

exec breeze stop

-- pipeline.hcl --
pipeline "release" {
  environments = ["staging"]

  stage "deploy" {
    type          = "deploy"
    fans_out      = true
    required_role = "deployer"
    timeout       = "10s"
    command       = ["/bin/true"]
  }
}
